commonIssues: Tolerates drought better than wet feet; soggy or poorly drained soil leads to stem and root rots — let the soil dry somewhat between deep waterings.
Over-fertilizing produces lush foliage at the expense of blooms; marigolds are low feeders — mix a balanced fertilizer in at planting and feed only sparingly afterward.
Spider mites can flare up in hot, dry weather; aphids, slugs, leafminers, whiteflies, and thrips are occasional pests.
Spent, rain-soaked flowers can develop gray mold (Botrytis); deadhead regularly to prolong flowering and reduce disease.
Tender warm-season annual: killed by frost; plant out only after the last spring frost once soil has warmed to about 18 C (65 F).
Compact bushy habit (15-45 cm) needs no staking; pinch young plant tips for bushier growth.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Marigolds. Need full sun all day for season-long bloom; tolerate drought better than being overly wet, so soil must be well-draining; pH 6-7; general-purpose fertilizer with equal N-P-K incorporated at planting only — extra feeding reduces bloom (low feeders); plant after soil reaches 65 F / frost danger passes; bloom up until frost; deadheading optional but encourages bloom; pinch young plants for bushiness.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — How to Grow and Care for Marigolds in South Carolina. Full sun (6+ hours daily); about 1 inch of water per week once established, watered deeply once weekly with soil allowed to dry somewhat between waterings; well-drained loamy soil pH 5.5-7.0; French types 6 in - 3 ft tall, spaced 8-10 in apart; suitable for containers; plant after last spring frost; blooms early summer until hard frost; pests include spider mites, slugs, leafminers, aphids, whiteflies, thrips; fungal stem/root rots and gray mold; deadhead for continued bloom.

University of Wisconsin-Madison Horticulture Extension — Marigolds. Full sun (6-8 hours direct); moderate water with even moisture but no waterlogging; well-drained soil pH 6-7; low feeders not requiring heavy fertilization; direct-seed once soil exceeds 65 F (~18 C) or transplant after frost danger; deadhead spent blooms; French marigold roots release alpha-terthienyl, toxic to root-knot nematodes.

RHS — Tagetes patula (French marigold). Hardiness H2 (tolerates low temperatures of about 1-5 C but not freezing — frost tender); full sun; moderately fertile, moist but well-drained to well-drained soil (clay, loam, or sand); height and spread 0.1-0.5 m in one year; suitable for patios and containers; water freely in dry spells; deadhead regularly to prolong flowering; generally pest- and disease-free.

Confidence note: No extension/RHS source gives exact container volumes for Tagetes patula; recommendedMinLiters (4 L, roughly a 6-8 in pot for one compact plant), idealMinLiters (7.5 L) and idealMaxLiters (20 L, multi-plant trough) are conservative estimates anchored to RHS/Clemson 'suitable for containers' and the plant's 15-45 cm size. wateringProfile.baseIntervalDays (3) interprets Clemson's 'about 1 inch per week, deeply once weekly, dry somewhat between' for smaller container volumes; hotWeatherMultiplier and rainSkipThresholdMm are care-engine estimates. feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays (45) is a deliberately sparse container-feeding cadence consistent with UMN/UW 'low feeder, fertilize at planting only' guidance, not an explicitly published interval. heatStressC (35) is a conservative estimate — French marigolds bloom through summer heat and no source states an explicit heat-stress threshold.
